title: Fabric elements on a scrapbook page
subtitle: Adhering fabric swatches to paper without puckering or bleed-through
excerpt: Fabric swatches, ribbon, and small cloth scraps add texture to a scrapbook page, but fabric does not behave like paper and most adhesives cause it to pucker or bleed through. This technique covers three methods for flat, clean adhesion depending on fabric weight and project type.
sourceNotes: Fabric-on-paper scrapbooking is a contemporary craft with no pre-1928 documented source. The adhesion methods described here are synthesised from general paper and textile craft practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fabric with a thin coat of diluted PVA brushed onto its back and allowed to dry. The dried PVA layer holds the cut edges of the fabric so it can be handled and cut as if it were stiff paper.
The unravelling of fibres along a cut edge of woven fabric. Fraying threads lift and spread over time, eventually obscuring the intended shape of the fabric element.
An adhesive join created by ironing fusible web between two surfaces. The heat melts the web's adhesive layer and bonds the two pieces permanently without any liquid adhesive.

content.text: Adding a fabric swatch to a scrapbook page means working with a different material. Woven fabric stretches slightly under pressure, its cut edges fray, and it absorbs liquid adhesives unevenly. The fix for most of these problems is to treat the fabric before you stick it down. Treating it makes it behave more like paper.

items.name: Fabric scraps
items.substitutions: Cotton and linen are the most stable choices. Avoid knits and open-weave fabrics; they stretch and fray readily.

items.name: Craft scissors
items.qty: 1, dedicated to paper and fabric work, not kitchen scissors

items.name: Double-sided tape
items.substitutions: Optional; the fastest method for small, flat fabric pieces.

items.name: Fusible web (iron-on)
items.substitutions: Optional; the most secure method for heavier or textured fabrics.

items.name: Washi tape
items.substitutions: Optional; for framing fabric panels on the page.

content.text: This is the most reliable method for most weights of fabric. Mix PVA with water in a ratio of roughly 1:2 (one part PVA to two parts water). Brush this mixture onto the back of the fabric in a thin, even coat with the paste brush. Set the fabric flat on a piece of non-stick surface (a plastic folder or waxed paper) and allow it to dry completely; at least thirty minutes.

can be cut cleanly with scissors, and its cut edges do not fray. The PVA layer prevents liquid adhesives (if you use any for the mounting step) from soaking through. To mount the treated piece: apply full-strength PVA to the scrapbook page, not to the fabric, and press the fabric down onto it.

For small, flat pieces of medium-weight woven fabric, double-sided tape is the fastest clean method. Apply strips of tape across the back of the fabric piece, covering the area fully (gaps allow the centre of the fabric to rise slightly). Peel the backing from the tape and press the fabric onto the page. Press firmly from the centre outward.

The cut edges still fray with this method. Seal them first: run a thin line of undiluted PVA along the cut edge with the tip of the paste brush, let it dry until clear, and then apply the tape. The PVA seal prevents

Fusible web creates the strongest bond and works best for heavier or textured fabrics. Cut a piece of fusible web slightly smaller than the fabric piece. Place the web onto the back of the fabric and iron it on using a medium heat and a pressing cloth between the iron and the web. Let it cool. Peel the backing paper away. Position the fabric on the scrapbook page and iron again through the pressing cloth, this time bonding the fabric to the page.

is permanent and will not peel. The heat also seals the cut edges, stopping fraying. Note: fusible web adds stiffness to the fabric; lightweight cotton remains flexible, but heavier fabrics may become rigid.

items.symptom: The fabric puckers in the centre after it dries
items.cause: The liquid adhesive was applied to the fabric rather than the page, causing the fabric to absorb moisture unevenly.
items.fix: Apply PVA to the page surface, not the fabric. Press from the centre outward. Weight under a flat board while drying.

items.symptom: PVA bleeds through to the front face of the fabric
items.cause: The PVA was too thin or was applied to the front side by accident.
items.fix: Mix PVA at 1:2 with water and apply only to the back. A very dilute mix soaks through thin fabrics; use a slightly thicker mix for fine cotton.

items.symptom: The fusible web lifts at the edges after cooling
items.cause: The iron temperature was too low or the pressing time was too short.
items.fix: Iron for 8-10 seconds (not a sliding motion) with firm pressure. For thick fabrics, press from both sides.

items.cause: The edges were not sealed before mounting.
items.fix: Apply a thin line of undiluted PVA along every cut edge and allow it to dry clear before mounting. Or use the fusible-web method, which seals the cut edges as part of the bonding process.

content.text: Combining fabric swatches with washi tape borders creates a mixed-media panel that reads as a textured, layered composition on the page. A narrow strip of washi tape pressed along the edge of a fabric piece ties the material to the page surface and adds a colour contrast. Narrow ribbon lengths can be adhered flat with double-sided tape and treated the same way as fabric panels.

Pages with fabric elements are bulkier than standard pages. Allow extra spine space when planning the album; a stack of ten fabric-mounted pages may require 10-15 mm of additional spine width compared with plain paper pages.
